title: SEGMENT 4 — Discovery: Twort, d'Hérelle, and the dysentery patients of 1917

lines.text: SEGMENT 4 — Discovery: Twort, d'Hérelle, and the dysentery patients of 1917

The discovery of bacteriophages is one of those stories where two people arrive at roughly the same observation, independently, within a year or two of each other, and then spend decades arguing about who got there first.
It's a recurring pattern in science — penicillin, the structure of DNA, evolution, calculus — and phage discovery fits the pattern.
The first observation, in print, is from Frederick Twort, a British bacteriologist, in 1915.

Twort was trying to grow vaccinia virus on agar plates, and he kept running into a strange problem: bacterial colonies that contaminated his plates would sometimes develop little glassy patches in them — patches where the bacteria seemed to have dissolved.

He took those patches, filtered the contents through a porcelain filter that should have removed any bacteria — the filter's pores were too small for bacterial cells to pass — and the filtrate, applied to fresh bacterial colonies, would produce more glassy dissolution.
Whatever was doing the dissolving was smaller than a bacterium.
It could be propagated.
It killed bacteria.
Twort wrote a careful, hedged paper proposing that he might have discovered an "ultramicroscopic virus" or perhaps an enzyme — he wasn't sure — and then the First World War interrupted his work.
He didn't follow up.
The paper sat largely unnoticed for years.

Two years later, in 1917, Félix d'Hérelle — a self-taught French-Canadian microbiologist working at the Pasteur Institute in Paris — independently rediscovered the phenomenon, and he had no doubts at all about what he was seeing.
D'Hérelle was studying dysentery patients during a French Army outbreak.
He was culturing Shigella from their stools, and he noticed the same kind of clear patches that Twort had seen — what we now call plaques — appearing on his bacterial lawns.
Crucially, he noticed something else.
When he tracked the plaques day by day in patients, the appearance of the plaques in the stool culture coincided with the patient's recovery.
The patients who developed the most plaque-forming activity in their stools were the patients getting better.
D'Hérelle put the pieces together with confidence.
There was an invisible agent, smaller than a bacterium, that ate Shigella, and it was helping the patients recover.
He named it the bacteriophage — the bacteria-eater.
D'Hérelle then did something Twort had not done — he developed the technique that turned plaques into a quantitative assay.
You take molten agar, you mix in your bacteria so it'll grow into a confluent lawn when it sets, you spread it on a plate, and you spot dilutions of the phage suspension on top.

Where each individual phage particle lands and successfully infects a bacterium, it triggers a local lytic cascade — the infected cell bursts, releasing dozens of progeny, which infect adjacent cells, which burst, and so on outward — and after overnight incubation, that one initial infection has produced a clear circular hole, a plaque, visible to the naked eye in the otherwise uniform bacterial lawn.
Count the plaques, multiply by the dilution factor, and you have a precise count of how many infectious phage particles were in your starting suspension.
This is the plaque assay, and it is still, in 2026, the gold standard method for counting phages.
It's also a beautiful example of how a single biological event — one phage particle infecting one cell — can be amplified into a macroscopically visible signal.
One particle equals one hole.
Pause.
That kind of single-molecule sensitivity, achieved with no fancy equipment, on a petri dish, in 1917, was a hundred years ahead of its time.

D'Hérelle was a remarkable, somewhat eccentric figure — never finished a formal degree, taught himself microbiology, traveled the world chasing infectious disease outbreaks — and he had absolutely no doubt that his discovery was going to revolutionize medicine.
He believed, from the start, that bacteriophages were therapeutic agents.

He started doing what we'd now call compassionate-use experiments — applying phage preparations to dysentery patients, plague patients, cholera patients — and in many cases reporting dramatic recoveries.
He set up phage therapy clinics in France, in India, in Egypt.
He founded a phage research institute in Tbilisi, Georgia — which we'll come back to — alongside the Soviet microbiologist Giorgi Eliava.

He was decades ahead of the field, and he was also, like many decades-ahead-of-the-field people, somewhat hard to take seriously, because he overstated his results, ignored confounders, and was bad at the controlled-trial methodology that the field was developing.
The phage therapy he pioneered worked sometimes, didn't work other times, and nobody could quite figure out the rules.
That ambiguity — does this stuff actually work?
— set the stage for the next chapter of the story, in which phage therapy got eclipsed by antibiotics.

But for the moment, hold onto the picture: 1917, a French-Canadian autodidact at the Pasteur Institute, looking at clear plaques in Shigella lawns, correctly identifying invisible particles smaller than bacteria as the predator, inventing the plaque assay, and going off to treat dysentery patients with phage preparations.
That's the founding act.
Everything that follows — molecular biology, phage therapy, phage display, CRISPR — rests on that first observation.
